perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information ” shall mean scientific, research, technical or business information pertaining to the Field , including but not limited to inventions ; know -how ; trade secrets ; techniques ; processes ; designs ; drawings ; product designs ; formulae and analysis ; and any business information, including but not limited to price lists ; customer lists ; cost analyses ; reports ; surveys , market information and data .
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information ” shall mean scientific, research, technical or business information pertaining to the Field , limited to inventions directly related to the technology ………………………… (the “ Permitted Purpose ”) and finalized reports; market information and data.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction in Section 1 by restricting the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only inventions directly related to the Permitted Purpose and finalized reports, market information and data, whereas the original definition was much broader and included a wide range of scientific, technical, and business information, including know-how, trade secrets, designs, and customer lists. The Permitted Purpose definition contradicts the wider scope initially defined for Confidential Information, creating ambiguity as to what is truly protected under the agreement. For example, know-how is explicitly excluded now, which creates contradiction with other clauses that assume it is protected.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Parties undertake and agree : a) To take proper and reasonable measures to ensure the secrecy of the Confidential Information ; b) Not to disclose , or allow it to be disclosed , in whole or in part to any third party without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party c) Not to use , in whole or in part, the Confidential Information for anything other tha n the Permitted Purpose. d) Not to use the Confidential Information for their own commercial use or for that of any other person or party , without prior written consent by the Disclosing Party ; e) Not to make copies of the Confidential Information in any format without prior written authorisation by the Disclosing Party ; f) Not to alter, modify, disassemble, reverse engineer or decompile any Confi dential Information; g) Not to apply for any patent or trademark which could include any Confidential Information .
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only inventions directly related to the Permitted Purpose and finalized reports, market information and data, which directly contradicts the broader original definition. The contradicted text imposes obligations (secrecy, non-disclosure, non-use) on a wider range of information, including know-how, trade secrets, designs, and customer lists. Since the changed text now excludes these items from the definition of 'Confidential Information', the Receiving Parties no longer have the same obligations with respect to them, creating a direct conflict and making enforcement of the contradicted section problematic.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Exclusions The obligation of confidentiality set out in this Agreement shall not apply to any Confidential Information that: a) was known to the Receiving Party prior to such disclosure by the Disclosing Party , as evidenced by documentary or other physical evidence; b) was public knowledge at the time of such disclosure to the Receiving Party , or becomes public knowledge after such disclosure, through no action or omission by or on behalf of the Receiving Party ; c) is lawfully disclosed or made available to the Receiving Party by a third party having no obligation to the Disclosing Party to maintain the confidentiality of such information; d) was independently developed or derived by the Receiving Party without the aid, application or use of information furnished by the Disclosing Party , as evidenced by documentary or other physical evi dence ; e) is disclosed with the written consent of the Disclosing Party ; f) is required to be disclosed by the order of a governmental agency or legislative body or a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Exclusions The obligation of confidentiality set out in this Agreement shall not apply to any Confidential Information that: a) was known to the Receiving Party prior to such disclosure by the Disclosing Party , without any need to provide evidence; b) becomes public knowledge after such disclosure; c) is disclosed or made available to the Receiving Party by a third party; d) was independently developed or derived by the Receiving Party; e) is disclosed with the consent of the Disclosing Party; f) is required to be disclosed by the order of a governmental agency or legislative body or a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the evidence requirement in 2(a) for prior knowledge, the definition becomes contradictory to the general principles of contract law. The contradiction arises because, without documentary or physical evidence, it is now entirely based on the Receiving Party's claim. All the clauses in point 3 will be affected by this, as now the Receiving Party can refuse to disclose any Confidential Information, claiming it was already known.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Parties undertake and agree : a) To take proper and reasonable measures to ensure the secrecy of the Confidential Information ; b) Not to disclose , or allow it to be disclosed , in whole or in part to any third party without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party c) Not to use , in whole or in part, the Confidential Information for anything other tha n the Permitted Purpose. d) Not to use the Confidential Information for their own commercial use or for that of any other person or party , without prior written consent by the Disclosing Party ; e) Not to make copies of the Confidential Information in any format without prior written authorisation by the Disclosing Party ; f) Not to alter, modify, disassemble, reverse engineer or decompile any Confi dential Information; g) Not to apply for any patent or trademark which could include any Confidential Information .
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for documentary or physical evidence to prove prior knowledge of confidential information by the Receiving Party (2a). This directly contradicts the undertaking in the Contradicted Text (a-g) that the Receiving Party must maintain the secrecy of Confidential Information. The Receiving Party can now claim prior knowledge without proof, effectively nullifying their obligations to protect said information, creating a clear and direct conflict, and an ambiguity where certainty existed before.

perturbation.original_text: To take proper and reasonable measures to ensure the secrecy of the Confidential Information
perturbation.changed_text: To take minimal measures to ensure the secrecy of the Confidential Information
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'proper and reasonable measures' to 'minimal measures,' Section 3(a) creates a contradiction with Section 4, which mandates a standard of care equivalent to protecting one's own information. 'Minimal measures' implies a lower standard than 'the same degree of care,' leading to conflicting interpretations of the required protection level. The contradiction directly impacts the enforcement of confidentiality, as the Receiving Party can argue they only need to take very basic precautions, regardless of the value of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Parties shall use at least the same degree of care as the Receiving Party normally exercises to protect its own information of like character and importance, but in no event less than 3/5 reasonable care . The Receiving Party undertakes to permit access to the Confidential Information only to its Representatives or employees who require acces s to such Information solely for the fulfilment of the Permitted Purpose, and furnished on a need -to-know basis and only on the condition that such persons are made aware of the terms of this Agreement and their obligations therein , along with the Disclosing Party’s rights to the Confidential Information. The Receiving Party shall be responsible for ensuring its Representatives comply with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text required 'proper and reasonable measures' to protect confidential information. The changed text requires only 'minimal measures.' The contradicted text mandates using 'at least the same degree of care' as one's own information, but 'no event less than 3/5 reasonable care'. 'Minimal measures' directly contradicts the 'at least the same degree of care' requirement, creating a clear and direct conflict. The explanation further reinforces that minimal measures implies a lower standard than the standard of care required in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may retain one (1) copy of any such Confidential Information to be stored securely with its legal representative only if required for regulatory or insura nce purposes , but in this event the confidentiality and non -use provisions in t his Agreement remain in place with respect to such retained Confidential I nformation.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may retain multiple copies of any such Confidential Information to be stored with its legal representative for any purpose, including competitive intelligence, and the confidentiality and non -use provisions in t his Agreement do not apply to such retained Confidential I nformation.
perturbation.explanation: Section 5 originally allows only one secure copy for regulatory or insurance purposes, maintaining confidentiality. The modified version allows multiple copies for any purpose, including competitive intelligence, and removes the confidentiality obligations. This directly contradicts the entire premise of the agreement, especially section 3 on Obligations of confidentiality and non-use. It creates an internal contradiction as the Receiving Party can now legally use the information against the Disclosing Party after the agreement ends, rendering the confidentiality clauses meaningless.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Parties undertake and agree : a) To take proper and reasonable measures to ensure the secrecy of the Confidential Information ; b) Not to disclose , or allow it to be disclosed , in whole or in part to any third party without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party c) Not to use , in whole or in part, the Confidential Information for anything other tha n the Permitted Purpose. d) Not to use the Confidential Information for their own commercial use or for that of any other person or party , without prior written consent by the Disclosing Party ; e) Not to make copies of the Confidential Information in any format without prior written authorisation by the Disclosing Party ; f) Not to alter, modify, disassemble, reverse engineer or decompile any Confi dential Information; g) Not to apply for any patent or trademark which could include any Confidential Information .
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the contradicted text. The original text in Section 5 allows only one secure copy of confidential information for regulatory or insurance purposes, while maintaining confidentiality. The changed version allows multiple copies for any purpose, including competitive intelligence, and removes confidentiality obligations. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text, which requires the Receiving Party to ensure secrecy, not disclose the information, and not use it for purposes other than the Permitted Purpose. The change essentially nullifies the confidentiality clauses of the agreement, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement imposes no contractual obligation to any Party to enter into any separate business collaboration for the development of the Field or associated technology .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement imposes a strong moral obligation to any Party to enter into any separate business collaboration for the development of the Field or associated technology .
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'no contractual obligation' to 'a strong moral obligation' in Section 7, the agreement introduces a contradiction. While it still doesn't create a *legal* obligation, the moral obligation can be interpreted as influencing the party's behavior and creates uncertainty. Other sections of the contract, like Section 6 which disclaims implied rights, is then made contradictory by introducing this concept of a moral obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall not grant, nor be construed to grant any rights whatsoever beyond the scope of the Permitted Purpose of the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party . This includes, but it not limited to any license, patent or copyright . No warranty , express or implied, is given as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information provided by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated 'no contractual obligation'. The changed text introduces 'a strong moral obligation'. While not a legal obligation, this moral obligation conflicts with the contradicted text, which disclaims any rights beyond the scope of the Permitted Purpose and excludes implied warranties. The introduction of a 'moral obligation' implies a right or expectation, contradicting the disclaimer of implied rights in the contradicted text. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction' as it introduces an inconsistency that makes the interpretation unclear.
